macro_segments.title: Opening remarks and introduction to Menachot; parallels with Zevachim

micro_segments.title: Good month greeting and preview of Menachot masechet
Structure of Menachot: grain sacrifices and related topics including Tefillin, Mezuzah, Tzitzit

macro_segments.title: The first Mishna: validity and fulfillment of intent in grain offerings

micro_segments.title: All grain offerings taken with wrong intent are valid but do not fulfill the owner's obligation
Exceptions: sin offering and Sotah offering where wrong intent fully invalidates
Unexplicated intent (stamah) is considered correct; explicit wrong intent invalidates

macro_segments.title: Four services and double intent scenarios in grain offerings

micro_segments.title: The four services paralleled between animal and grain sacrifices: taking, placing in vessel, conveying, burning
Scenarios of double intent: lishman v'shelo lishman; sin offering versus freewill offering
Detailed example: oven mincha with intent for fried mincha invalidates

macro_segments.title: The force of the word 'ela' and implications for validity

micro_segments.title: Question: why use 'ela' instead of 'v'lo'; emphasis that the offering itself is fully kosher
Cannot add additional wrong intentions; principle that once declared valid, proper intent remains required
Proof from Rava: even a slaughtered animal with wrong intent still requires proper sprinkling intent

macro_segments.title: Neder versus Nedava and the principle of fulfilling obligations

micro_segments.title: Verse analysis: distinction between personal obligation (neder) and object designation (nedava)
If performed according to original vow, counts as neder; if varied in intent, counts as nedava
Free-will offering remains valid but does not exempt from obligation; wrong intent does not grant license for further violations

macro_segments.title: Gemara opens: is our Mishna according to Rabbi Shimon?

micro_segments.title: Gemara suggests Mishna may not follow Rabbi Shimon; citing Baraita of Rabbi Shimon allowing wrong intent by grain offerings
Rabbi Shimon's reasoning: grain offerings are self-evident in physical form, unlike animal sacrifices
Examples: dry offering with intent for oil-mixed offering; fried with intent for baked offering

macro_segments.title: Debate over whether intention redefines the act or is discarded by physical reality

micro_segments.title: Two interpretations: either intention is dismissed as silly, or intention only defines acts when physical reality is neutral
Intention meaningful only if it defines the quality of action; physical reality takes precedence when evident
Stamah (unexplicated) is valid because act is defined by the offering's inherent identity, not by thought

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's resolution: distinguishing between physical form and halakhic category

micro_segments.title: Difference between stating intent about physical vessel versus halakhic designation of offering type
Intent about physical form (machavat vs. marcheshes) is meaningless; intent about offering category is meaningful
Possible reconciliation: Mishna discusses halakhic categories, Rabbi Shimon discusses physical form

macro_segments.title: Rabbah's answer: distinguishing wrong owner from wrong offering type

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon concedes wrong owner (shinui baalim) invalidates; debates wrong offering type (shinui kodesh)
Mishna's case of sin offering versus freewill is about offering type, not owner; therefore could fit Rabbi Shimon

macro_segments.title: Contradiction within Rabbi Shimon's positions on wrong intent and its effects

micro_segments.title: First position: wrong intent by grain offering does not invalidate because physically self-evident
Baraita quoting Rabbi Shimon: sin offering with wrong intent is invalid; freewill offering with wrong intent does not fulfill obligation
Need to reconcile: what cases does Rabbi Shimon allow wrong intent to matter

macro_segments.title: Abiyi's challenge: if Gemara compares mincha to chatat and asham across the board, why limit only to wrong owner

micro_segments.title: If verses make full comparison, all intentions should invalidate, not just wrong owner
Response: Rabbi Shimon uses logical reasoning (sevara) to limit scope of verse based on when thought is not obviously contradicted

macro_segments.title: Analysis of bird sacrifices: intent about location versus intent about offering type

micro_segments.title: Olah of bird at top with intent for chatat of bird: intent should be meaningless due to location
Melika can occur anywhere on altar; squeezing of blood can occur at top after sprinking at bottom
Even wrong intent is not self-evidently false for bird sacrifices in same way as grain offerings
Reverse case: chatat of bird at bottom with intent for olah is obviously wrong
Tosafot raises cases where intent about animal sacrifices is self-evident and should not invalidate

macro_segments.title: Conclusion: Rabbi Shimon's position on when intention matters depends on physical self-evidence

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon agrees wrong intent matters for wrong owner (shinui baalim) since not obviously contradicted
Rabbi Shimon disagrees for wrong offering type (shinui kodesh) when self-evident; agrees when not self-evident
Discussion continues next session on other interpretations and further cases

topical_tags.term: Rashi (on intent being articulated, not just in mind)
Rava (on continuing violations after initial wrong intent)
Rav Ashi (distinguishing vessel form from halakhic category)
Rabbah (distinguishing wrong owner from wrong offering type)
machbat vs. mercheshet (fried vs. bubbly oil mincha)
leshem baalim (for the sake of different owner)
ma'aseh mochiah / physical reality defines act
olah / olat ha'of (elevation offering / bird elevation)
elu lo yered (if one doesn't go down [to lesser offering])
minchas marcheshes (specific type of fried mincha)
lo alu l'ba'alim leshem chova (did not fulfill obligation for owners)
chesed and gemilut hasadim context (widow law)
melika (pinching the neck of bird sacrifice)
